description: Product design decisions and rationale captured from planning chats — informs readmap.json

entries.topic: Why three pillars — Curiosity vs Connection vs Conservation
entries.summary: Product design question: distinguish Connection and Conservation enough for meaningful epics, or combine?
entries.decision: Keep three pillars. Curiosity → Connection → Conservation is stronger than Curiosity → Conservation alone.

curiosity.psychology: Knowledge — I know this reef exists.

connection.psychology: Identity — This is my favorite reef.
connection.nature: Passive but personal — not action yet

conservation.psychology: Responsibility — I want to protect this reef.

why_not_merge.cost_of_merging: Loses entire class of Connection features (saved places, dive journals, checklists, community photos, personal collections) — those are not conservation features; they are what make people care.
why_not_merge.museum_analogy: Zoos, aquariums, and national parks invest in interpretation before asking for donations or volunteers. A new diver clicks 'What animals live here?' before 'Volunteer for reef restoration.' After 20 minutes exploring, they are receptive to 'This reef has lost 40% of its coral. Here's how you can help.'

epic_clarity.curiosity_epics: Beach database, species encyclopedia, condition maps, search, articles
epic_clarity.connection_epics: Profiles, favorites, logs, sightings, galleries, journals, ecosystem timelines
epic_clarity.conservation_epics: Cleanup map, volunteer coordination, citizen science, donations, eco directory, impact dashboards

entries.ui_implication: Homepage leads with Curiosity (discovery). Connection and Conservation are separate nav destinations; conservation also woven into beach pages as 'Protect this place' after user has explored.
